# Team Sizing & Availability Modeling
<!-- Generated By: Claude Code (Claude Opus 4.6) -->

> Dimension 7 of 8 | See [../citations.md](../citations.md) for full source details

## Key Finding

AI-augmented teams are shrinking from 6-10 to 2-5 humans, but individual productivity gains do NOT translate 1:1 to organizational throughput. The real bottleneck is not code generation (25-35% of SDLC) but review, integration, and deployment. Sprint velocity recalibration should use conservative multipliers (1.1x-1.3x).

---

## Team Size Evolution

| Source | Traditional Size | AI-Augmented Size | Ref |
|---|---|---|---|
| AKF Partners (MVP/feature) | 5-8 | 2-3 humans + AI | [59] |
| AKF Partners (complex/legacy) | 8-12+ | 4-6 humans + AI | [59] |
| Google (per Stack Overflow) | 30-60 per capability | Smaller components | [60] |
| Stack Overflow reporting | 10+ | 2-3 "concentrated capability" | [60] |
| Orosz (Pragmatic Engineer) | 6-8 specialists | 3-4 full-stack engineers | [46] |
| OpenAI Codex Team | N/A | ~40 total (1 PM, 2 designers, ~37 engineers) | [61] |

**Consensus:** 4-6 humans remains the recommended size for complex/enterprise work. 2-3 is viable for MVPs with strong AI tooling.

---

## Brooks's Law in the AI Era

### AI as Tool (Copilot-style) [45]

- **Partially circumvents** Brooks's Law
- Not a new team member -- a "force multiplier"
- Reduces communication overhead by reducing number of humans needed
- Enables smaller, more agile teams

### AI as Autonomous Agent [45]

- **Strengthens** Brooks's Law
- IS a new "teammate" requiring coordination
- Hidden costs:
  - **Context engineering** replaces "ramp-up" for new team members
  - **Review overhead**: AI code is "syntactically perfect but logically flawed"
  - **Debugging overhead**: "black box" code with no human mental model

> "Brooks's Law is not only upheld in the age of autonomous agents; it is strengthened." -- Forret [45]

### Brooks's Surgical Team Model Applied to AI [45]

Maps well onto agentic workflows:
- Chief programmer (human or lead agent) defining structure
- Specialist agents for tests, documentation, migrations
- Single integrator for merging and verification
- "This model scales. Unconstrained parallelism does not."

---

## The Productivity Paradox

Individual gains consistently fail to translate to organizational throughput:

| Source | Individual Metric | System Metric | Ref |
|---|---|---|---|
| Faros AI | +21% tasks, +98% PRs | No DORA improvement | [27] |
| DORA 2024 | Individual flow up | -1.5% throughput, -7.2% stability | [21] |
| Bain 2025 | 10-15% coding speedup | ~10% system ceiling | [38] |
| CMR meta-analysis | Varies by skill | No aggregate gains found | [18] |
| Six studies convergence | Various | ~10% system-level ceiling | [27] |

**Root cause:** Amdahl's Law -- the slowest part of the pipeline dictates overall speed [27]. AI accelerates coding while review, testing, and deployment remain unchanged.

---

## Capacity Planning

### Individual Capacity Adjustments

Based on evidence across studies:

| Developer Profile | AI Multiplier | Confidence | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| Junior, routine tasks | 1.3x - 1.5x | Moderate | [2][5][26] |
| Mid-level, mixed tasks | 1.1x - 1.3x | Moderate | [2][37] |
| Senior, familiar codebase | 1.0x (no adjustment) | High | [3] |
| Any dev, unfamiliar/greenfield | 1.2x - 1.4x | Low-Moderate | [1][4] |

### Sprint Velocity Formula

No standardized AI-adjusted formula exists. Proposed framework:

```
Effective_Capacity = Team_Size x Hours_Per_Sprint x Focus_Factor x AI_Multiplier x (1 - Rework_Factor)

Where:
  Focus_Factor: 0.6 - 0.9 (standard, accounts for meetings/interruptions)
  AI_Multiplier: 1.0 - 1.5 (by role/task profile, see table above)
  Rework_Factor: 0.05 - 0.15 (accounts for AI-introduced bugs and review overhead)
```

**The Bain Correction [38]:**
```
Total_AI_Impact = AI_Coding_Speedup x SDLC_Coding_Share
Example: 1.30 x 0.30 = 1.09 (9% total delivery improvement)
```

### Sprint Velocity Recalibration

- Plan at 75-85% of the average of the last 3 sprints [sprint planning research]
- Start with 1.1x multiplier on historical velocity and adjust based on observed data [27]
- Recalibrate quarterly or after major tool/process changes
- Account for increased review burden (+91% review time per Faros [27])
- Expect 2-4 weeks productivity drop during AI tool learning curve [27]
- AI-driven planning improves sprint commitment completion from 60-70% to 85-90% (est.) [sprint planning research]

---

## Availability Modeling

### Traditional Focus Factors

| Team Profile | Focus Factor |
|---|---|
| Experienced, stable, minimal interruptions | 80-90% |
| Typical team with regular meetings | 70-80% |
| High support responsibilities / many meetings | 60-70% |

### AI-Specific Adjustments

- Developers spending median 2 hrs/day (25% of workday) interacting with AI [22]
- 59% running 3+ AI tools in parallel [60]
- 27% of AI-assisted work at Anthropic "wouldn't have been done without AI" [83] -- AI enables NEW work, not just faster existing work
- Jevons Paradox: developers work MORE hours, not fewer [49]

---

## Emerging Organizational Patterns

| Pattern | Description | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Minimum Viable Engineering Team (MVET) | Single engineer + multiple AI agents | [44] |
| Concentrated capability teams | 2-3 devs doing what 10+ did | [60] |
| AI Integration Lead | New role for evaluating/recommending AI tools | [60] |
| Full-stack convergence | Specialists become generalists with AI | [46] |
| Surgical team (modernized) | Human architect + specialist AI agents | [45] |

## Gaps and Limitations

- No published ratio data for "orchestrators" vs "traditional developers" exists
- No standardized availability model accounts for AI context switching patterns
- The team size reduction claims (6-10 to 2-5) are based on expert analysis and early case studies, not longitudinal research
- Sprint velocity adjustment formulas are proposed frameworks, not validated against production data
- The 50% workforce reduction prediction [46] is one expert's opinion (Steve Yegge), not a consensus view
